title: Critical quench dynamics of Wegner's $\mathbb{Z}_2$ gauge model: a geometric perspective
authors: Ramgopal Agrawal, Leticia F. Cugliandolo, Marco Picco
abstract: Wegner's $\mathbb{Z}_2$ gauge model is the earliest formulation of pure lattice gauge theory and predicts the topological nature of the confinement-deconfinement transition. In three dimensions ($D=3$), the equilibrium critical behavior of the model is understood in terms of geometrically defined objects, namely loop excitations and Fortuin-Kasteleyn (FK) clusters. This work investigates the critical quench dynamics of this model from a geometric perspective, following quenches from both a high-temperature percolation phase and the zero-temperature ground state. Using time-dependent finite-size scaling analysis, we find that the critical non-equilibrium relaxation of the percolation order parameter is governed by a dynamical exponent $z_{\rm p} \simeq 2.6$, consistent with that associated with the energy density, $z_{\rm c}$. Importantly, the value of $z_{\rm p}$ is robust with respect to the initial quench condition and the choice of geometrical objects. Furthermore, we provide a detailed characterization of the kinetics of different geometrical objects during the evolution from the percolation phase. Notably, we observe that the quench dynamics obeys dynamic scaling in terms of a growing lengthscale, $ξ_{\rm p}(t) \sim t^{1/z_{\rm p}}$, despite the absence of a local order parameter.
summary: This work analyzes the non-equilibrium dynamics of the $\mathbb{Z}_2$ gauge model by tracking the relaxation of geometrically defined structures like FK clusters and line loops. Using finite-size scaling, the authors determine a universal dynamical exponent $z_p \simeq 2.6$ that governs the approach to criticality. This confirms that geometric measures are highly effective tools for characterizing critical dynamics in systems with global order parameters.
main_problem: The paper investigates the critical quench dynamics of Wegner's $\mathbb{Z}_2$ gauge model, focusing on how geometric observables relax towards equilibrium after a sudden change in temperature.
main_result: The critical relaxation of percolation observables is governed by a dynamical exponent $z_p \simeq 2.6$, which is robust across different quench protocols and matches the energy density exponent $z_c$.
method: The study employs time-dependent finite-size scaling analysis and analyzes the scaling of cluster size distributions $N(s, t)$ for various geometric objects.
model_or_system: The system is Wegner's $\mathbb{Z}_2$ gauge model on a 3D cubic lattice, which exhibits a topological confinement-deconfinement phase transition. Dynamics are studied by quenching from high-temperature or zero-temperature states to the critical point.
key_observables: Percolation order parameter ($P(t)$), energy density ($e(t, L)$), and the size distribution of clusters $N(s, t)$ for FK clusters and line loops.
important_parameters:
Dynamical exponents ($z_p, z_c$), critical exponents ($ eta_p, 
u_p$), and the lattice size $L$.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on dynamic scaling hypotheses, assuming that observables can be collapsed onto universal scaling functions dependent on a characteristic lengthscale $\xi(t) \sim t^{1/z}$.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the collapse of scaled energy density and percolation observables onto universal scaling functions, confirming the predicted power-law decay of cluster size distributions $N(s, t) \propto s^{-\tau_p}$ at late times.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the geometric nature of the model's critical behavior, then applying finite-size scaling to measure the dynamical exponents ($z_p$ vs $z_c$), and finally analyzing the size distribution $N(s, t)$ to confirm dynamical scaling across different cluster types.
why_interesting: The finding that geometrical observables yield a dynamical exponent consistent with the thermal critical exponent, despite the absence of local order parameters, provides a powerful, non-standard diagnostic tool for studying critical dynamics in gauge theories.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
